description: A systematic approach to building robust, maintainable software by explicitly defining and enforcing behavioral contracts between components.
principles: Declare explicit contracts for all interfaces.
Enforce preconditions before execution and postconditions after.
Preserve class invariants across all operations.
Restrict inheritance to strict subtype relationships.
Isolate interfaces from implementations for independent evolution.
Validate external inputs rigorously at system boundaries.
Prioritize reuse through composition over deep inheritance hierarchies.

workflow.purpose: Establish clear expectations before coding begins.
guidelines: List all preconditions, postconditions, and invariants for each public method.
Document expected input ranges, state requirements, and guaranteed outputs.
Review contracts with stakeholders to align on behavioral guarantees.
pitfalls: Vague or incomplete requirements that leave edge cases unhandled.
Over-specifying constraints that restrict legitimate usage patterns.

workflow.purpose: Translate abstract contracts into executable safety checks.
guidelines: Place precondition checks at method entry and postcondition checks at exit.
Verify invariants at the start and end of every public operation.
Use automated assertion libraries to minimize manual boilerplate.
pitfalls: Forgetting to restore object state before exiting, breaking invariants.
Placing checks inside loops or critical paths, causing performance degradation.

workflow.purpose: Structure reuse and polymorphism without fragility.
guidelines: Apply inheritance only when the subclass strictly satisfies the parent contract.
Prefer composition for sharing state or behavior across unrelated classes.
Keep hierarchy depth shallow to simplify debugging and maintenance.
pitfalls: Deep inheritance trees that amplify changes across unrelated branches.
Using subclassing to share implementation rather than behavioral substitution.

workflow.purpose: Ensure system-wide consistency and contract compliance.
guidelines: Run full contract checks during development and testing phases.
Disable runtime checks in production builds to optimize performance.
Automate postcondition verification with integration tests for critical paths.
pitfalls: Silent contract violations that mask defects until production.
Performance bottlenecks from unchecked assertion overhead in release builds.

workflow.purpose: Enable future extensions without breaking existing code.
guidelines: Define abstract base classes with stable contracts for extension points.
Document how subclasses may override or extend base behavior.
Isolate volatile logic behind interfaces to limit ripple effects.
pitfalls: Tight coupling between core modules and extension hooks.
Hidden dependencies that violate open-closed expectations.

checklist: All public interfaces declare explicit preconditions and postconditions.
Class invariants are verified at the start and end of every public method.
Inheritance is restricted to strict subtype relationships without state duplication.
External inputs are validated at system boundaries before internal processing.
Contract enforcement checks are disabled in production builds for performance.
Interfaces are fully decoupled from concrete implementations.
Automated tests cover postcondition verification for critical paths.
Extension points are documented and isolated from core logic.

vocabulary.definition: A software engineering approach where components specify obligations (preconditions), guarantees (postconditions), and unchanging rules (invariants).
A condition that must hold true for every instance of a class throughout its lifetime, except during transient state changes.
A principle ensuring that objects of a subclass can replace objects of a superclass without altering program correctness.

heuristics: Prefer composition over inheritance for code reuse.
Fail fast: let contract violations surface immediately during development.
Keep contracts as simple as possible while remaining complete.
antipatterns: Silent contract violations: ignoring preconditions or postconditions in production without logging.
Contract inflation: adding excessive checks that degrade performance without adding safety.
Inheritance abuse: using subclassing to share implementation rather than behavior.

examples.context: Building a payment processing module with external API integrations.
examples.outcome: Contract enforcement catches malformed requests early, preventing downstream corruption and simplifying debugging.

examples.context: Refactoring a legacy monolithic class into a hierarchy of specialized handlers.
examples.outcome: Strict subtype rules prevent fragile base class issues, enabling safe parallel development.
